The problem involves two dormitories named Oakwood and Maplewood.
We know that the total number of students in both dorms is ninety-six.
Initially, the number of students in each dorm was not the same.
Five students moved from Maplewood to Oakwood to equalize the populations.
After this move, both dorms have an identical number of residents.
Since the total is ninety-six, each dorm must have forty-eight students now.
This means Oakwood currently holds forty-eight students after the transfer.
To find the original number, we must reverse the movement of five students.
We subtract the five incoming students from the current count of Oakwood.
Forty-eight minus five equals forty-three students originally in Oakwood.
Maplewood must have started with fifty-three students to allow this transfer.
If Maplewood had fifty-three, moving five leaves it with forty-eight.
This confirms that the final counts are indeed equal at forty-eight each.
The sum of forty-three and fifty-three is exactly ninety-six students.
The math checks out perfectly for the given conditions in the prompt.
We can confidently state the initial population of Oakwood was forty-three.
